The string/suffix matching worries me. It's not a reliable test for the cases we're looking for--it may catch types that happen to end in one of the suffixes being checked w/o there being any relationship between the suffix and the type (e.g. imagine an object type with the token `pkg:module:fooArgs`). I'd prefer that we move away from string matching/suffix matching and towards using the structure of the types themselves (i.e. the `hcl2.Type` values and their annotations)` to make these checks.

No need to change the PR, but just for future reference for a change like this, it probably makes sense to cherry-pick the part that is on master (the `update.sh` script change) as one commit and then include a separate commit for the proto changes.

That makes it more clear which parts are branch-specific and which parts are just copied from master.

I'd like to see an interface here where we do something safe & performant by default for external callers, and handle the edge case where there are multiple external packages that would alias via the option you added to set the external package map.

We should guard writes to the default (global) map by a mutex. I don't think codegen programs are ever long-lived and tend to involve repeatedly querying the same packages over and over. If memory usage is an issue, we can add some code to evict unused entries later.

However, I think if you're going to make this change, we need to do something to make its consistency enforced. I'm not sure if that means making it explicit in some documentation or if it could be added to pre-commit. You're more of a pre-commit expert to know if it's possible.

LG overall however there is some functionality missing.

First, all commit methods should support this, including `Batch.commit()`.
Secondly, we usually call commit on behalf of the user which means they will not be able to pass the options to the commit call themselves. This means we need to provide alternate ways of passing in the options. e.g.
```
database.run_in_transaction(transaction_func, request_options={"priority": "PRIORITY_LOW"})
```
```
for database.batch(request_options={"priority": "PRIORITY_MEDIUM"}) as batch:
    batch.insert(table, columns, data)
```

Additionally, you should update the PR description to give context on the feature and how it should be used. For example, it should mention that all requests are run as `PRIORITY_HIGH` by default and so this feature is to deprioritize tasks.

This is a good idea. But I'm suspicious of the robustness of the escaping (which also applies to the custom Docker command).

In theory what we want to do is impossible; you can't represent an arbitrary string as a YAML multiline string, which is where this eventually goes. If we wanted to get as close as possible, we'd have to Bash-escape out the newlines and other stuff YAML can't hold, which I don't think anything knows how to do.

But we could at least check for newlines and bail out.

Hey @vballoli,

thanks a lot for this PR. We discussed this internally and have some comments on this:

1. `MovingMNIST` is a video dataset, i. e. the individual frames have a strong temporal relation. Currently you are treating it as an image dataset. We should return 4D tensor representing a single video (`channels x depth x height x weight`) rather than an image with multiple channels (`channels x height x weight`). This also means that we shouldn't treat the frames individually for the transforms.
2. You only implemented the test split, since this is the only generated data provided by the author. Since the authors also provide the code to generate the sequences (see for example the [implementation by `tensorflow`](https://github.com/tensorflow/datasets/blob/b9deaa07f567ad3211850a1e5c1ae993a1a03409/tensorflow_datasets/video/moving_sequence.py)), it might be beneficial to also provide a train split. If we do this, we need to decide if we want to generate the sequences ahead-of-time or on-the-fly. Depending on how large the training split will be, the former might be to much on a burden on the memory (even the test split is ~1GB and resides completely in memory).
3. Although the authors split the sequences of two with 10 frames each, this should not be required since `MovingMNIST` is marketed as an unsupervised dataset. This is especially important if we generate the trainings data with a deviating number of frames. IMO we should simply return a single video. Maybe we could provide a (static) method that implements this split by the authors.

Let us know what you think and if you are willing to continue working on this.
Thanks!

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [ ] Field kwargs
	* [ ] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [ ] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [ ] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [ ] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

> I think it was correct to make the second commit but I'm never fully confident about this

You are right! For the most part, running with tornado and straight asyncio are interchangeable. One of the key differences, though, is that _calling_ an asyncio coroutine only creates an object to be run, no execution actually happens, whereas calling a tornado  coroutine schedules it immediately. Calling a tornado coroutine is more equivalent to `asyncio.ensure_future(coroutine())`, which does the same thing. Awaiting is another, more common way to schedule a coroutine on the current loop.

So the behavior is ever-so-slightly changed, in that now the handler will actually wait for the previously un-awaited calls to `emit` to complete before moving on, but I think that's probably what we already wanted, anyway. We should get more sensible error reporting, if nothing else.

You note correctly. In my experience, there is often a meaningful
smoothing difference between `0.99` and `0.999`, and I often like to set

value logarithmically: say, apply `log_2(1 - x)` to the current value.

since I often find myself fiddling in the very extremes.

Here's my summary of the problem and solution -- just wanted to clarify my understanding from the PR description:
1. Paused trials come from scheduler decisions. Once a scheduler decides a trial should be paused, it should basically be ignored by the Tune controller, until the scheduler decides that it's this trial's turn to run again.
2. Previously, it was possible to go directly from paused -> staged -> pending (set by `_schedule_trial_actor`) -> running.
    - With this fix, it's now only possible to go from pending -> staged -> running.
    - So, the only route for a paused trial to resume would be: paused -> pending -> staged -> running.

Due to point 2, [should this line be an](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/860a8c9c7b70daaea75e0172a4ccdd23f81146ad/python/ray/tune/execution/tune_controller.py#LL545C12-L545C12) `assert trial.status == PENDING` instead of setting it again? Hi @Islast - thank you for taking the time to draft this PR.  

I'm not sure the original documentation is in error.  

Since `ContentsManager` is essentially an abstract base class from which all contents manager subclasses ultimately derive, this update is saying that no matter what concrete form of `ContentsManager` will be used, instances of _that_ class will use _this_ `pre_save_hook`.  Whereas the current doc states that only instances of `FileContentsManager` (or its subclasses) will use _this_ `pre_save_hook`.

Which `ContentsManager` class is used by the server is configured via `NotebookApp.contents_manager_class`.  That said, server extensions are free to enlist the services of other `ContentsManager` subclasses where those extensions may not want a `pre_save_hook` configured for that particular class - for example.

Because the original doc entry is in the context of an example and the example is functionally correct, I think it might be best to retain the current doc.  Since the [default `ContentsManager` class is actually `LargeFileManager`](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/notebookapp.py#L1382-L1383), perhaps an update to that would be more appropriate - since one should probably target the most-leaf class to avoid side-affecting other subclasses.

**Design question:** At the API level, could we consolidate `prefetch_blocks` and `async_fetch_batch` with a single `prefetch_batches` argument that gives the number of batches to asynchronously prefetch?

## Motivation

I think this will be a lot cleaner from an end-user perspective, with a single argument controlling prefetching behavior that has clear batch-based semantics. We ultimately care about **pipelining upstream batch preparation with downstream batch computation**, and both prefetching batches over the network and background batch slicing/concatenation/formatting fall under the former, so having a single batch-based argument controlling how many batches we want to prepare while a single batch is being processed seems like better UX. IMO, the object store transfer prefetching should probably be an implementation detail in facilitating a generic "how many batches to we want to prepare in the background on this worker" API, similar to the semantics of the [`tf.data` prefetching API](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/data/Dataset#prefetch).

## Do We Need Two Knobs?

Since this seems like nicer UX, the only reasons that we'd want to have two knobs (that I can think of) are:
1. We want to have different levels of prefetching for object store transfers compared to background slicing/concatenation/format conversion.
2. The prefetching unit for the two args should be different (e.g. batch vs. block).

(1) could be true. If we assume that we're not hitting NIC bandwidth or CPU bottlenecks during deserialization or batch prep, then prefetching object store transfers is ultimately limited by the node's object store capacity, while background slicing/concatenation/format conversion is ultimately limited by the node's worker heap, which are separate resource pools with different capacities. This is further complicated by format conversions leading to memory inflation. E.g. for a Pandas batch format UDF on Arrow blocks executed on a node that has equal object store and worker heap allocations, you could imagine that we want to prefetch 8 batches to the object store, but only do 1 background batch slice and format conversion since the Pandas format conversion results in a DataFrame that's 4x larger than the Arrow data (and the UDF will need to at least allocate an output in the worker heap as well); this inflation could easily happen for boolean data, for example, which is bitpacked in Arrow but is not in Pandas.

However, this may be a non-issue if:
* the Python worker heap is typically the bottleneck rather than the node's object store (I think this is true for transformations/trainers that don't use a NumPy/Arrow narrow waist), or;
* we can rely on Ray Core's object pull manager to do proper backpressure on object transfers (this should be true).

The user can set `prefetch_batches` tailored to whatever eliminates compute resource stalls subject to a Python worker heap cap, and object transfer prefetching will do a best-effort fulfillment of `prefetch_batches`, subject to Ray Core's backpressure on object transfers when the node's object store pressure gets high. I'm thinking that this might make (1) a non-issue.

(2) is the status quo, but I think that the current object store prefetching API would be better expressed in terms of batches (this has been a long-standing TODO), so I don't think that (2) is a concern.

## Semantics and Implementation

For `.map_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics, except that the buffer size is configurable. Down the line, we might have this arg control the number of blocks that are prefetched by actor workers under the actor compute strategy, rather than that being a strategy config.

For `.iter_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics along with exposing the buffer size, _and_ would control how many blocks are prefetched into the object store on the consumer/worker node. Namely, if we know the number of rows in a block from the readily-available block metadata (this should be defined in the common case), we would prefetch `N` blocks such that `N` is the smallest number of blocks that satisfies `prefetch_batches`, i.e. we choose an `N` such that `sum(b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N-1]) < prefetch_batches * batch_size <= sum(b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N])`. If the block metadata doesn't have the number of rows in the block (e.g. the dataset is a lazy read of CSV files, for which we don't know the number of rows until the entire files has been read), then we can prefetch one block at a time.

What do y'all think? cc @jiaodong

In the case that we will use fit, probably we could inherit from the base estimator since it estimates from data.

```python
class ValueDifferenceMetric(BaseEstimator):
    def __init__(self, k=1, r=2):...

    def fit(self, X, y):
        # learning unique classes here

    def pairwise(self, X, Y=None):...

```

On the other hand, another option would be to require the data in the init.

```python
class ValueDifferenceMetric:
    def __init__(self, X, y, k=1, r=2):...

    def pairwise(self, X, Y=None):...

```

Additionally we could implement the callable API 

```python
vdm = ValueDifferenceMetric(...)
distance = vdm(x1, x2)

```

Design wise, I would be in favor for the following in order to use the DistanceMetric API but is way to slow.

```python
class ValueDifferenceMetric:
    def __init__(self, X, y):...

    def __call__(self, x1, x2, k=1, r=2):... 

vdm = ValueDifferenceMetric(X,y)

metric = DistanceMetric.get_metric(vdm)
metric.pairwise(X)

# or

knn = KNearestNeighbors(metric=metric)

```

All these assuming that `X` is ordinal encoded with ints.

> I never used `coveralls` myself, I'm not sure if that should be part of GH Actions task.

> Open questions:
> 1. Should `BSPCompileRequest` take multiple `FieldSet` values? This would allow the language backend to merge compilation via `CoarsenedTargets` since the ;language backend would see all of the relevant field sets together. I'm leaning toward that as the better approach than the current implementation in this PR.

In practice, I'm not sure that it will matter...? `CoarsenedTarget` is deterministic regardless of entrypoint, so if you have two files within one cycle, they will always "become" the same `CT`, and trigger the same compilation. As the API is now, that would mean two roots depending on the same inner compilation node.

Depending on how you're rendering outputs, merging might prevent redundant rendering of compilation outputs...? Our logging of `CompileResult` will already dedupe, but if you need to send specific per-request output, merging might make more sense.

> 2. Is there a way to get absolute paths into the Coursier cache? (So BSP can provide the scalac classpath to IntelliJ.) If not, then that resolution will need to be written out just like classfiles are currently by the PR.

There is... it's always possible to just "leak" a path. But note that that won't work in the context of remote execution (the named_cache would only exist on the remote host). I'm not sure that it's worth a special case. Note too that if BSP supports jars (rather than loose classfiles) as artifacts for that case, it would be surprising if it didn't support them for per-target compilation.

> 3. Big question is what should be in `classpath` returned by the `buildTarget/scalacOptions` BSP method. I'm going to ask on the intellij-scala Discord about it. At the very least, I am going to split the classfiles directories of different resolves. (And we will probably need to write out the jars of a resolve to a stable location just like for scalac classpath if there is no way to get paths into the Coursier cache that can be shared with IntelliJ, but that is not for this PR.)

> 1. does this create new problems?

It is a new dependency for non-core functionality, since we're not worrying about CSS-based XSS for legacy browsers in 5.x.

It could make UI redress and privacy or data leaks easier. Bleach doesn't have visibility into how its output is displayed so it can't fix UI redress (I should document this). I like the new "type" argument. However, if I correctly understood the intended changes, they are in gross contrast what usual prediction functions involving link functions do. Sorry for this late input. I think it is important to consider it anyway.

According to `predict.glm()`, type "response" returns values on the scale of the response.  A Poisson regression with log link would return positive values, a binary logistic regression would return probabilities.

Type "link" means, on the other hand, that predictions are made in the link space. For a logistic regression, it would be the logit of the probabilities, for a Poisson regression the log expected counts etc. 

**LightGBM seems to intend to do it in the exact oppositite**. Thus, when working with objectives involving a log link (Poisson, Gamma, Tweedie), the new `predict()` would suddenly return values on the link space instead on the response scale.

My suggestions: 

- The default type is "response". Binary logistic will return probabilities. 
- There is either "raw" or "link" but not both. For historic reasons, I tend to "raw", but "link" is fine as well.
- There should be a type = "class" in order to get the majority class for those objectives where it makes sense.
- Type "leaf" and "contrib" are okay.

I don't think `ThreadPoolExecutor` is a good choice here. Because of the GIL, the standard Python interpreter will only ever run one thread at any given time. Thus spreading the work over several threads will never be faster than just doing all the work in the main thread. In fact, it is quite a bit slower, especially with the default arguments for ThreadPoolExecutor (before py38 it defaults to 5 * numProcessors threads). 

If I understand the problem correctly, then AWS lambdas and celery workers don't allow multiprocessing. And they are designed to execute one thread of code.

Wouldn't it be simpler to just add a `max_workers` argument to `generate_clks`. In case of 1, just run everything in the main loop, otherwise pass it on to ProcessPoolExecutor. 

(This would also allow us to tame clkhash if needed. Currently it just grabs all available cores.)

Mostly LGTM, the big thing that we need resolve IMO is the semi-lazy read behavior. Semi-lazy reading (eagerly reading the first block/file) has been confusing for users, results in redundant reading when there's stage fusion or conversion to a pipeline, and I think we should try to get rid of it when switching to lazy execution by default, if possible. I think that it would be much better if we had fully lazy mode and eager mode, where semi-lazy execution is an execution optimization that is enabled when we determine we only need to compute a subset of blocks; these semantics should translate pretty well to our new execution planner and streaming execution model (e.g. limit pushdown, metadata peeking, streaming iteration).

Instead of always computing the first block, I think that we should make the read fully lazy, and if the user immediately calls `ds.schema()` right after reading, we only then trigger reading of the first block (and only if the schema isn't already available from e.g. file metadata).

I _think_ it should be pretty straightforward to move this logic to the `ExecutionPlan`, since we can have `ExecutionPlan.schema()` (and other ops that only require the first block) trigger semi-lazy execution with e.g. a certain flag set, and if that flag is set AND the plan only consists of a read stage AND the input blocks are a lazy block list, we trigger minimal computation of that read stage.

I think you can simplify your code a lot if you would not duplicate the entire query in you if statements, but instead build up the filter clause based on the if statements.
something like done here as well: https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/catalogue/managers.py#L39

The code would look something like this then:
```
_filter =  models.Q(includes=self) # start with filter clause that always applies
_exclude_filter = models.Q(excludes=self) # also build and exclude
 if self.classes.exists():
    _filter |= Q(product_class__classes=self)
if Product.objects.filter(parent__includes=self).exists():
    _filter |= Q(parent__includes=self)
    _exclude_filter != Q(parent__excludes=self)

# etc ...

return Product.objects.filter(_filter).exclude(_exclude_filter)
```
Something like that. You can make the code a LOT shorter if you do it like that and perhaps keep it in the single method?

Hello @hemanth-silabs 

Thank you for this contribution, it's an important step towards more secure products! Good work!

I have an overall question about architecture. Right now the code is sliced with ifdefs in many places to choose between PSA API and mbedTLS API.

Haven't you considered using only the PSA API (whenever possible)? As far as I understand, we can update the mbedTLS version to one that supports PSA API (e.g. 2.25 from TF-M 1.3) - I have it done on a branch and can contribute. Since for non-TF-M type of devices, PSA API implementation translates code to mbedTLS API under the hood anyway. For example the `psa_hash_update` will call `mbedtls_sha256_update_ret` - [code](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/v2.25.0/library/psa_crypto.c#L2655). The only caveat is that for such non-TF-M devices, we would need to implement ITS API for the key-by-reference feature.

As an additional argument, I believe mbedTLS will not have "old" mbedTLS API in due time anyway.

Thanks for submitting a PR but as it stands currently, this hurts UX in the general case. This captures the output of the Black invocation and never reemits it. That's already not great, but it gets worse. If Black errors out for some reason, the developer reading the logs will have no idea what's wrong since the logs will be almost entirely empty. There are two ways of fixing this:

- Reemit the captured output (always or on a non-zero return code; I'd prefer the former)  
- Tee or somehow duplicate the output stream so it can be captured _and_ printed to the console as usual
 
The second could preserve the colour output and allow for live feedback (unlike the first option), however, I have never done this before so no clue whether this is possible. ASNI codes make it sound really annoying.

I know this Action's output could be improved in general, but that's dependant on GitHub fixing their own infrastructure (which will never happen IME), but if it's possible let's not make it worse.

Also, the general convention for GitHub Actions are to use dashes instead of underscores.  [Here's an example.](https://github.com/marketplace/actions/close-stale-issues).

Finally, I'm not keen on using using numbers as boolean, we could use `true` / `false` instead. I can go either way on this point though.

I know this is a lot of words, but I do really mean it that I appreciate the effort you've put into this. I'm just one of those "let's right it the first time" folks (un)fortunately :)

This looks good to me in terms of API and implementation (though I agree with most of Samuel's suggestions about some of the details).

I think some clear documentation with:

* Some motivation for why this is necessary
* (Closely related) when you should define this method for a custom type/annotation
* _How_ to use it / how the mechanics of it work in some concrete examples (Decimal is okay, though a better example might not depend on any understanding of pydantic-core)

would go a long way toward making it clear why this is worth adding. I'm not _1000%_ convinced this is the best possible way to handle the "inversion of control" for types to decide how annotations apply to them. BUT I think it's carefully considered and sensible, and I haven't been able to come up with anything better. And I can't think of any scenario it wouldn't be able to handle.

(Honestly, I think structuring the documentation akin to a lightweight PEP would probably help with understanding of this functionality both internally (i.e., devs) and externally (i.e. (advanced) users).)

----

I think the `__get_pydantic_core_schema__`, `__prepare_pydantic_annotations__`, and `__get_pydantic_json_schema__` triumvirate should make it possible to extend pydantic to work ergonomically with just about any validation semantics for custom/third party types that anyone could desire — provided a willingness to get hands dirty with the annotation implementations.

I'm giving the concepts introduced in the earlier PR a more thorough review this time.  

Looking at `MappedPartitionsResult`, I wonder if we can change the names in a way that avoids introducing new terminology:
- `invalid_partitions_mapped_to` -> `required_but_nonexistent_partition_keys`.
- `MappedPartitionsResult` -> `UpstreamPartitionsResult`, unless we think there's some notion of nonexistent / invalid that's meaningful in the downstream case?

Then for the constructs in this PR:
- `ParentPartitionsResult` -> `ParentsPartitionsResult` (parents is plural to match the naming of `get_parents_partitions`, where parents is plural to indicate that there are multiple assets)
- `valid_parent_partitions` -> `parents_partitions`
- `invalid_parent_partitions` -> `required_but_nonexistent_parents_partitions`

Combined, these would avoid introducing a new concept of a "valid / invalid" partition.
